blocks.text: Chapter 4.
The Heuristics: Anchors, Availability, and Linda
The ideas in 30 seconds
Small samples produce extreme results in both directions, yet System 1 spins causal stories about what is really statistical noise — a bias Kahneman calls the law of small numbers.
Any number you see before estimating pulls your estimate toward it: anchoring moves judgments by roughly 55% of the anchor's size on average, and experts are barely more resistant than novices.
We judge frequency and risk by how easily examples come to mind, which is why media coverage, emotion, and availability cascades can inflate minor hazards into national panics.
Representativeness — judging probability by similarity to a stereotype — makes us ignore base rates, as in the Tom W. problem, unless the statistic is given a causal story.

The Linda problem shows that a plausible, detailed story can seem more probable than a bare fact it contains, violating elementary logic; adding detail always lowers probability while raising believability.
Averages and prototypes dominate sums: in Hsee's dinnerware study, adding broken pieces to a larger set lowered its perceived value — less is more when items are judged one at a time.
Small Samples Lie, and We Believe Them Anyway

Counties with the lowest kidney-cancer rates in the US are mostly rural, sparsely populated, and in traditionally Republican states. Clean living? The counties with the highest rates fit exactly the same profile. The real explanation is sample size: small populations swing to extremes — high or low — purely by chance. Nothing about rural life causes or prevents kidney cancer.

This is the law of small numbers: our intuitive belief that small samples resemble the population they came from. They don't — small samples yield extreme outcomes far more often than large ones. The Gates Foundation and others spent roughly $1.7 billion promoting small schools after studies found small schools overrepresented among top performers. Small schools were also overrepresented among the worst performers, for the same statistical reason; if anything, large schools produce better results.

Even experts fall for it. Kahneman and Tversky's first joint paper showed that professional research psychologists routinely chose sample sizes too small to detect the effects they were studying — trusting intuition over computation and risking studies that could not confirm true hypotheses.

The deeper problem is that System 1 is a machine for suppressing doubt and constructing coherent causal stories. It does not track sample size; it treats "the sample I saw" as the truth (WYSIATI, from the previous chapter). It also sees patterns in randomness. Sequences of coin flips that alternate look "more random" than runs, though runs are equally likely. Londoners during the Blitz saw deliberate targeting in what analysis showed was a random bombing pattern. Gilovich, Vallone, and Tversky found the "hot hand" in basketball to be a cognitive illusion: streaks in shooting data are what chance alone produces. The practical rule: when a striking pattern appears in a small sample, your first hypothesis should be luck, not cause.
Anchors Pull Every Estimate — Yours Included

In a classic demonstration, Kahneman and Tversky spun a rigged wheel of fortune that stopped only at 10 or 65, then asked subjects what percentage of UN members are African nations. Those who saw 10 guessed 25% on average; those who saw 65 guessed 45%. A transparently random number moved a factual estimate. This is the anchoring effect: exposure to a number biases subsequent estimates toward it.

Anchoring works through two mechanisms. One is deliberate but lazy: System 2 starts at the anchor and adjusts, stopping too early — the reason people misdate Gandhi's death when first asked whether he died before or after age 144. The other is automatic: the anchor acts as a prime, making System 1 selectively retrieve evidence consistent with it. A high listing price makes you notice a house's virtues; a low one, its flaws.

The effect is quantifiable. The anchoring index is the shift in estimates divided by the difference between anchors; a fully anchored answer scores 100%, an immune one 0%. Typical values hover around 55%: asked about the tallest redwood with anchors of 180 versus 1,200 feet, visitors gave mean answers of 282 versus 844 feet. Professionals are not exempt. Real-estate agents touring the same house with different fabricated listing prices produced valuations with a 41% anchoring index — barely better than business students' 48% — while insisting the list price hadn't influenced them.

Anchors work even when arbitrary and even in the wild: a supermarket sign reading "limit of 12 per person" doubled soup purchases from 3.3 to 7 cans; caps on damage awards anchor negotiations; the first number on the table dominates any haggle. Defenses exist but require System 2: recognize an outrageous anchor as an attack, deliberately argue against it — "think the opposite" — or walk away rather than counter within its range.
Availability: What Comes to Mind Feels Common

The availability heuristic substitutes an easy question for a hard one: instead of "how frequent is this?" we answer "how easily do instances come to mind?" Ease of retrieval tracks frequency imperfectly — it also tracks drama, recency, and personal experience. That is why spouses' estimates of their own share of housework sum to well over 100%: your own efforts are more available than your partner's. Plane crashes feel likelier just after one is in the news.

Norbert Schwarz added a twist: the experience of fluency matters more than the content retrieved. People asked to list six instances of their own assertiveness later rated themselves more assertive than people asked to list twelve — because retrieving twelve is hard, and the struggle itself is read as evidence ("if I can't think of examples, I must not be assertive"). The effect reverses when the difficulty is explained away (say, by background music): then people go by content. Engaged, busy, or expert judges lean on content; those coasting on System 1 lean on fluency.

Availability distorts public risk perception systematically. Paul Slovic's research shows lay judgments of hazards are driven by emotion — the affect heuristic — and by media coverage rather than statistics: subjects judged accidents and disease equally lethal though disease kills about 18 times more people, and rated accidental death more likely than death by stroke, which actually kills nearly twice as many. Slovic argues "risk" has no single objective definition; whoever controls the definition controls the policy.

Kuran and Sunstein named the runaway version an availability cascade: a minor story triggers coverage, coverage triggers public alarm, alarm triggers more coverage, and policy overreacts. The 1989 Alar scare — an apple-ripening chemical posing negligible risk — devastated the apple industry and, perversely, may have reduced fruit consumption. Sunstein wants policy set by expert cost-benefit analysis; Slovic counters that public fear, rational or not, is real and democracies must reckon with it. Kahneman splits the difference: cascades misallocate resources, but fear itself is a cost.
Representativeness: Stereotypes Beat Base Rates

Tom W. is described as intelligent but dull, orderly, corny, fond of sci-fi. Asked which graduate field he's likely in, people confidently pick computer science — ignoring that education and humanities enroll vastly more students. This is the representativeness heuristic: judging probability by similarity to a stereotype. Rankings of "probability" turned out identical to rankings of similarity; the base rate — prior odds before any evidence — was simply discarded, along with the fact that a thumbnail personality sketch is nearly worthless evidence.

Representativeness often beats chance (librarians are more often bookish), but it commits two sins: excessive willingness to predict rare outcomes from flimsy cues, and insensitivity to the quality of evidence — System 1 processes a dubious description as if it were true (WYSIATI again). The corrective is Bayesian discipline in two rules: anchor your judgment on a plausible base rate, and question how diagnostic your evidence really is. Weak evidence should leave you close to the base rate.

Chapter 16 explains when base rates do get used: when they support a causal story. In the taxicab problem, told that 85% of a city's cabs are Green, people ignore this statistical base rate when judging a witnessed hit-and-run. But told that Green cabs cause 85% of accidents — a causal base rate — they use it, because it creates a stereotype of reckless Green drivers. Causes trump statistics.

The dark corollary comes from Nisbett and Borgida: students taught the results of the famous helping experiment (in which most bystanders failed to aid a person having a seizure) still predicted that the pleasant individuals they saw on video had rushed to help. Statistics about people changed no minds. But students shown two individuals who didn't help correctly inferred the general statistic. People learn from surprising individual cases, not from surprising numbers — to teach statistics, show instances.
Linda and Less Is More: When Adding Detail Subtracts Truth

Linda is 31, single, outspoken, a former philosophy student deeply concerned with discrimination and social justice. Which is more probable: Linda is a bank teller, or Linda is a bank teller and active in the feminist movement? About 85-90% of undergraduates — including graduate students in decision science — chose the second. That is logically impossible: every feminist bank teller is a bank teller, so the conjunction cannot be more probable than either part alone. This is the conjunction fallacy: detail makes a scenario more plausible and representative while making it less probable. Stephen Jay Gould confessed that even knowing the answer, "a little homunculus in my head" kept insisting otherwise.

The fallacy shrinks when questions are framed as frequencies. Asked how many of 100 Linda-like women fit each description, most people get it right — concrete individuals cue set logic that percentages do not.

The same triumph of prototype over quantity appears in valuation. Christopher Hsee showed people dinnerware sets: Set A had 40 pieces, 9 of them broken; Set B had 24 pieces, all intact — A strictly contains B plus extras. In joint evaluation, everyone paid more for A. In single evaluation — each set seen alone — people paid $33 for B and only $23 for A. System 1 represents sets by averages and prototypes, not sums, so broken pieces poison the whole. Economist John List replicated this in a real market: a set of ten baseball cards plus three mediocre ones sold for less, when judged alone, than the ten alone. Less is more, and in scenario judgment, more detail is less probability.
In practice
When a small sample shows a dramatic result — a hot streak, a standout branch office, a viral study — assume chance first and ask for the sample size before accepting any causal story.

Before any negotiation or estimate, name the anchor out loud, then deliberately generate arguments for why the true value is far from it; if an opening offer is outrageous, refuse to counter within its range.
When judging how risky or common something is, replace "how easily can I recall examples?" with actual frequency data; be especially suspicious right after heavy news coverage of a vivid event.

Whenever you predict a category or outcome from a description (a hire, a diagnosis, a market call), write down the base rate first and adjust only as far as the quality of your evidence genuinely warrants.
Distrust detailed scenarios: each added specific makes a forecast more convincing and less likely, so strip predictions down to their essential claim before assigning a probability.
When valuing anything — an offer, a portfolio, a product bundle — evaluate by totals side by side with alternatives, not by the average impression of a single option seen alone.
